content.text: Index-as-key Pitfalls
Using the array index as a
key

prop is the most common mistake in React list rendering. It silences the missing-key warning while still allowing React to incorrectly match components across renders. The symptoms range from subtle state corruption to jarring UI glitches, and they often only appear once the list changes — making them easy to miss during development.

attrs.alt: Four failure modes of index-as-key: input mismatch, animation glitch, useEffect over-fires, focus lost
attrs.title: Index keys cause four distinct bug categories when list order changes

content.text: Why the index seems fine at first

Appending to the end of a list works correctly with index keys because appending doesn't shift any existing indices. The problem only surfaces when items are prepended, removed from the middle, or reordered. This means the bug is latent — the code looks fine in initial testing and breaks only when a user performs the triggering action.
Pitfall 1: input and form state corruption

This is the most visually obvious bug. When a user has typed into a form field and the list reorders, the typed value stays attached to the DOM node at that position rather than following the data item the user typed into.
The same bug appears with controlled inputs if the component state is held inside the list item component (not lifted up).
useState
in a child component is tied to that component instance. Index keys cause the wrong instance to be reused, so the state follows the position instead of the data.
Pitfall 2: animation and transition misfires

Animation libraries (Framer Motion, React Spring, CSS transitions via class changes) detect additions and removals by key. With index keys, removing item 0 from a 3-item list looks to the library like items 0 and 1 changed and item 2 was removed — so all three items animate, and the exit animation plays on the wrong (last) item instead of the one that was deleted.
Pitfall 3: useEffect runs for the wrong component
If a list item component has a
useEffect
that depends on its data (e.g. fetches details for its item), index keys cause the effect to re-run for all components after a prepend because each component at those positions now has different
item
props.
Pitfall 4: focus and scroll position lost

Browser focus and scroll position are tied to specific DOM nodes. When index keys cause React to destroy a node and create a new one at the same position (instead of moving the old node), focus is lost — the user's keyboard position jumps unexpectedly.
When index-as-key is safe (the complete checklist)
Index-as-key is safe if and only if all of the following are true. If any condition fails, use a stable ID.
Condition
Safe?
Example
List is never reordered, filtered, or items removed/prepended
Required
Static navigation links
List items have no local state inside them
Required
Pure display components with no inputs
Data has no stable IDs
Required
Rendering
['red','green','blue']
No animation library observing the list
Required
Not using AnimatePresence, TransitionGroup, etc.
Generating stable IDs when data doesn't include them
The fix is always to assign a stable ID when the item is created, not during rendering. Never generate IDs inside a
.map()
callback — a new ID on every render defeats the purpose:
ESLint rule: react/no-array-index-key
The
eslint-plugin-react
rule
react/no-array-index-key
warns whenever the array index is used as a key. Enable it to catch the pattern at authoring time rather than discovering it through a bug report:
The rule flags
key={i}
and
key={index}
patterns. It does not flag
key={item.id}
or other non-index expressions. Upgrading from warn to error is appropriate for codebases where dynamic lists are common.
Compound keys when IDs are not globally unique

Sometimes items have IDs that are unique within a category but not globally (e.g. item IDs restart from 1 in each category). Concatenate the category ID and item ID to form a globally unique key within the list:
Debugging index-key bugs in the browser
The React DevTools Profiler shows component re-mounts (orange outlines) vs updates (blue outlines). When you suspect an index-key bug:
Open DevTools → Profiler → start recording.
Prepend or reorder an item in the UI.
Stop recording and inspect the flame graph. With index keys you see all list item components highlighted (all re-rendered). With stable keys, only the new item is highlighted.
A component showing "Did not render" (grey) in the profiler means React skipped it — the sign that stable keys are working.
Testing for the bug
Pitfall 5: server-side rendering hydration mismatch
In Next.js and other SSR frameworks, React hydrates the server-rendered HTML by matching it to the client-rendered virtual DOM. Index keys cause mismatches when:
The list is dynamically filtered (e.g. by user role or locale) and produces a different length on client vs server.
The data is fetched client-side after hydration and replaces the initial (empty or skeleton) list.
With stable IDs, React can hydrate correctly even when the client and server render different slices of data. The key is the anchor, not the position.
Tracking the pitfall with React Strict Mode

React Strict Mode (enabled by default in development builds with Vite/Next.js) intentionally double-invokes effects and renders. This amplifies index-key bugs: effects fire twice, and any state corruption becomes immediately visible. If your list looks correct in production but glitches in development, index keys are a prime suspect.
Sorting state and re-render cost with index keys

When a user sorts a large list, index keys force React to patch every DOM node because every element appears to have changed. Stable keys let React identify that the same items simply moved — it calls
insertBefore
to reorder existing DOM nodes rather than updating their props. For a list of 200 items, this is the difference between patching 200 nodes and moving nodes without touching their content:
Real-world audit: finding index keys in a codebase
To audit an existing codebase for index-as-key patterns, search for the common patterns:
key={i}
key={index}
key={idx}
. In a terminal:

# Find index-as-key in JSX files
grep -rn 'key={\(i\|idx\|index\)}' src/

# Or with ripgrep
rg 'key=\{(i|idx|index)\}' src/ --type tsx --type jsx

# For each match, check:
# 1. Can the list be reordered, filtered, or prepended?
# 2. Do items have local state or uncontrolled inputs?
# 3. If yes to either, replace with a stable ID
The difference between key and id attribute
A common misconception is that
key
and the HTML
id
attribute serve the same purpose. They do not:
Attribute
Who uses it
Visible in DOM?
Scope
key
React's reconciler
No
Unique within sibling list only
id
CSS selectors, labels (
htmlFor
), URLs
Yes
Must be globally unique in document
You can (and often should) have both:
key
for React reconciliation and
id
for linking labels to inputs or for anchor navigation. Using the same value for both (when it is globally unique) is fine.
Migration: replacing index keys in an existing codebase
When migrating a large codebase from index keys to stable IDs, a phased approach prevents regressions:
Enable the ESLint rule as a warning
to discover all instances without blocking CI.
Categorise each instance:
static list (safe, suppress warning with a comment), dynamic list with no state (migrate now), dynamic list with state or inputs (highest priority).
Add IDs to the data model
at the source (API response transformer or
useState
initialiser). Avoid adding them in the render function.
Update key props
from
key={i}
to
key={item.id}
Write regression tests
that prepend an item and assert the existing items retain their state.
Upgrade ESLint rule to error
once all dynamic lists are migrated.
Virtual list libraries and key requirements

Virtual list libraries (react-window, TanStack Virtual) render only the visible rows and recycle DOM nodes as the user scrolls. They depend on stable, consistent keys to know which row maps to which data item. Index keys in virtual lists cause the recycled DOM node to show stale content from the previous row at that position — the same bug as prepend, but happening on every scroll event.
Summary
Index-as-key silences the warning but causes incorrect DOM reuse when the list order changes.
The four failure modes are: input/form state corruption, animation misfires,
useEffect
over-firing, and focus loss on reorder.
Index-as-key is only safe when the list is static, items have no local state, and no animation library observes it.
Assign stable IDs when data is created, not inside the
.map()
callback.
Enable the
react/no-array-index-key
ESLint rule to catch the pattern at authoring time.
Use compound keys (
`${categoryId}-${itemId}`
) when IDs are locally unique but not globally unique in the rendered list.
